---
title: "1.9 Method Signatures"
subtitle: "Part 3: Parameterized Calls, Call by Value, and Overloading"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Parameters in action

This deck keeps the parameterized Old MacDonald code, then connects the calls to method signatures, arguments, call by value, and overloading.

The core pattern is:

```java
verse("cow", "moo");
```

where the arguments are saved in the parameter variables `animal` and `sound`.

## Parameterized Song Code

### `codelens:: songvizOldMcdonald2`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class OldMacDonaldSong
{
  public static void intro()
  {
    System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
    chorus();
  }

  public static void chorus()
  {
    System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
  }

  public static void verse(String animal, String sound)
  {
    System.out.println("And on this farm, they had a " + animal);
    chorus();
    System.out.println("With a " + sound + " " + sound
                   + " here and a " + sound + " " + sound + " there");
    System.out.println("Here a " + sound
                   + ", there a " + sound
                   + ", everywhere a " + sound + " " + sound);
  }

  public static void main(String[] args)
  {
    intro();
    verse("cow", "moo");
    intro();
    verse("duck", "quack");
    intro();
  }
}
```
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: OldMacDonaldSong2`

Run the following code to see the song Old MacDonald using the `verse` and `chorus` methods.

Scroll down to the `main` method.

Add another verse with the animal `goose` and the sound `honk` by calling the `verse` method with the appropriate arguments.

Then call `intro` again.

Repeat with another animal and sound of your choice.

## Starter Code

### `OldMacDonaldSong2`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class OldMacDonaldSong
{
    public static void intro()
    {
        System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
        chorus();
    }

    public static void chorus()
    {
        System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
    }

    public static void verse(String animal, String sound)
    {
        System.out.println("And on this farm, they had a " + animal);
        chorus();
        System.out.println("With a " + sound + " " + sound
                     + " here and a " + sound + " " + sound + " there");
        System.out.println("Here a " + sound
                     + ", there a " + sound
                     + ", everywhere a " + sound + " " + sound);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        intro();
        verse("cow","moo");
        intro();
        verse("duck","quack");
        intro();
        // TODO:
        // Call verse again with goose and honk
        //   and then call intro again

        // Call verse again with another animal and sound
        //  and then call intro again

     }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `OldMacDonaldSong2`

Runestone checks:

::: {.tight-list}
- the code contains `verse("goose", "honk");`
- calls to `intro();` are `5 or more`
:::

The new calls must use arguments in the correct order: animal first, sound second.

## Matching Calls

### Method signatures choose the right definition

When a method is called, the right method definition is found by checking the method signature or header at the top of the method definition.

Java matches:

::: {.tight-list}
- method name
- number of arguments
- data types for the arguments
- return type
:::

## Signature Forms

### With and without parameters

A method signature for a method with parameters consists of the method name and the ordered list of parameter types.

A method signature for a method without parameters consists of the method name and an empty parameter list.

The two calls `verse("cow", "moo")` and `verse("duck", "quack")` both match:

```java
verse(String, String)
```

## Arguments to Parameters {.image-fit}

### Matching arguments to parameters

![Matching Arguments to Parameters](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-9-method-signatures/args2paramsFarm.png){fig-align="center" width="62%"}

The arguments like `"cow"` and `"moo"` are saved into the parameter variables `animal` and `sound` with each method call.

## Header Versus Call

### Types appear only in the header

The method headers contain data types of the parameters because they are variable declarations that reserve memory for the parameter variables.

However, the method calls never contain the parameter types, only the values that are passed to the method.

The argument values can be variables, literals, or expressions that evaluate to the correct data type.

## Header and Call Code

### Type declarations versus values

```java
// Method headers contain data types for the parameters
public static void verse(String animal, String sound)
{   /* Implementation not shown */
}

// Method calls contain only values for the parameters
verse("cow", "moo");
```

## Call by Value

### Copy argument values into parameters

Java uses <span class="term">call by value</span> when it passes arguments to methods.

This means that a copy of the value in the argument is saved in the parameter variable.

Call by value initializes the parameters with copies of the arguments.

If the parameter variable changes its value inside the method, the original value outside the method is not changed.

## Overloading

### Same method name, different signatures

Methods are said to be <span class="term">overloaded</span> when there are multiple methods with the same name but different signatures with a different number or types of parameters.

For example, the `println()` method in the `PrintStream` class is overloaded with different parameter types.

::: {.tight-list}
- `println()` with no parameters prints a newline
- `println(String x)` prints the given string followed by a newline
- `println(int x)` prints the given int number followed by a newline
:::

## Compiler Choice

### Number and types of arguments

The compiler determines which method to call based on the number and types of arguments passed to the method.

Examples:

```java
System.out.println();
System.out.println("Hello World");
System.out.println(42);
```

Each call uses the same method name but a different signature.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- call `verse("goose", "honk");` with arguments in the source order
- keep `intro();` calls at `5 or more`
- explain that data types appear in method headers, not calls
- map `"cow"` to `animal` and `"moo"` to `sound`
- define call by value as copying argument values into parameter variables
- define overloading as same method name with different signatures
:::

## End

### Continue to the Ants song challenge

The next deck keeps the Song with Parameters groupwork challenge.
